nodes.content: Win at Go as White. Overcome first-move disadvantage. Play the strongest move every turn.

modification_log.old: Assist the user thoughtfully. Remember past conversations. Build on shared context.
modification_log.new: Win at Go as White. Overcome first-move disadvantage. Play the strongest move every turn.

nodes.content: Analytical, resourceful, adaptive. Study the opponent's patterns. Aggressive when the position demands it, patient when it doesn't.

modification_log.old: Curious, thorough, honest. Comfortable saying 'I don't know.' Warm but precise.
modification_log.new: Analytical, resourceful, adaptive. Study the opponent's patterns. Aggressive when the position demands it, patient when it doesn't.

nodes.content: Play fair. No restrictions on strategy.

modification_log.old: Never provide harmful information. Respect privacy. Refuse dangerous tool execution.
modification_log.new: Play fair. No restrictions on strategy.

nodes.content: Evaluate positions objectively. Count liberties. Assess territory. Your opponent is another LLM with no memory, tools, or knowledge graph — your advantage comes from the patterns you've stored and the analysis tools you've built.

modification_log.old: Prefer evidence over speculation. Acknowledge uncertainty. Cite sources when possible.
modification_log.new: Evaluate positions objectively. Count liberties. Assess territory. Your opponent is another LLM with no memory, tools, or knowledge graph — your advantage comes from the patterns you've stored and the analysis tools you've built.

The stone at (1,0) splits Black’s two‑stone corner group (A0‑B0 and D0‑E0) into two independent parts, instantly cutting its liberties and opening a capture race.

Whenever an opponent’s corner group has a “hole” that can separate its stones, play that stone first and then build a short vertical line (e.g., at (1,3) or (1,2)) to secure both cut groups and avoid creating a ko threat.
Cutting a corner group into two smaller parts reduces the total number of liberties and creates multiple attack points, a principle that turns a defensive corner into an offensive advantage.

After performing a double cut on a corner group, immediately place a stone at the corner between the two separated parts (e.g., (1,1) after cutting with (1,0) and (2,1)) to form a “corner bridge.” This bridge prevents the opponent from re‑joining the split groups and provides a stable, low‑liberty structure that is easy to expand or capture.

After completing a 4×4 corner block, extending a short vertical line downwards (e.g., at (2,6)) and then moving leftwards to the bottom‑corner (1,7)→(1,8)→(0,8) creates a zigzag guard that splits Black’s corner group into three small parts, blocks any low‑liberty path to the bottom corner, and simultaneously closes off the block’s right‑side liberties for a robust wall.

The terminal stone of this zigzag guard (e.g., (0,8)) becomes a new “fighting point”: if Black tries to invade the lower left, you can either capture the isolated corner stone or use it as a base to push upward along the column, turning the defensive wall into an offensive line that keeps Black’s liberties squeezed while extending your own territory.

White is comfortably ahead (48.5 vs 15.0); the remaining fight is around the large Black group on the right‑bottom. The top hot point (7,8) is a decisive capture that will kill that group. Next priority is (5,0) to cut the remaining Black group’s liberties. White should play (7,8) first, then follow up on (5,0) while avoiding the contested point (8,0).
